stem: For the transformation \( x = [[a]]u^2 + ([[b]])v, \quad y = [[c]]u + [[d]]v^2 \), compute the Jacobian determinant \( \dfrac{\partial(x,y)}{\partial(u,v)} \) at the point \( (u,v) = ([[u0]], [[v0]]) \).
solution: The Jacobian is \( \det\begin{bmatrix} x_u & x_v \\ y_u & y_v \end{bmatrix} = \det\begin{bmatrix} 2\cdot[[a]]u & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & 2\cdot[[d]]v \end{bmatrix} = 4\cdot[[a]]\cdot[[d]]uv - [[b]]\cdot[[c]]. \) At \( ([[u0]],[[v0]]) \) this equals \([[answer]]\).
distractor_rationales: This adds \(x_v y_u\) instead of subtracting it (wrong sign in the determinant).
This forgets the factor \(4\) coming from \(x_u y_v = (2\cdot[[a]]u)(2\cdot[[d]]v)\).
This is the negative of the determinant (the two diagonal products were swapped).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: For the scaled-polar transformation \( x = [[a]]r\cos\theta, \quad y = [[a]]r\sin\theta \), compute the Jacobian determinant \( \dfrac{\partial(x,y)}{\partial(r,\theta)} \) at the point \( (r,\theta) = ([[r0]], [[theta0]]) \) (angle in radians).
solution: The Jacobian is \( \det\begin{bmatrix} [[a]]\cos\theta & -[[a]]r\sin\theta \\ [[a]]\sin\theta & [[a]]r\cos\theta \end{bmatrix} = [[a]]^2 r\cos^2\theta + [[a]]^2 r\sin^2\theta = [[a]]^2 r \), independent of \(\theta\). At \(r=[[r0]]\) this is \([[answer]]\).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the extra factor of \([[a]]\) that comes from scaling both \(x\) and \(y\) by \([[a]]\).
This drops the factor of \(r\) that comes from \(y_\theta = [[a]]r\cos\theta\).
This drops the scale factor \([[a]]^2\) entirely, keeping only the \(r\) from the standard polar Jacobian.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: For the linear transformation \( x = [[a]]u + v, \quad y = u + [[b]]v + w, \quad z = [[c]]w \), compute the Jacobian determinant \( \dfrac{\partial(x,y,z)}{\partial(u,v,w)} \). (Since the map is linear, this Jacobian is the same at every point.)
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the three diagonal entries \([[a]],[[b]],[[c]]\) directly, ignoring the off-diagonal \(1\)'s.
This makes a sign error inside the \(2\times2\) cofactor, using \(ab+1\) instead of \(ab-1\).
This has the overall sign flipped from the correct cofactor expansion.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The transformation \( x = [[a]]u + ([[b]])v, \quad y = ([[c]])u + [[d]]v \) is invertible. Using the inverse function theorem, find the Jacobian determinant of the inverse transformation, \( \dfrac{\partial(u,v)}{\partial(x,y)} \).
solution: By the inverse function theorem, \( \dfrac{\partial(u,v)}{\partial(x,y)} = \left(\dfrac{\partial(x,y)}{\partial(u,v)}\right)^{-1} \). Here \( \dfrac{\partial(x,y)}{\partial(u,v)} = [[a]]\cdot[[d]]-[[b]]\cdot[[c]] \), so the inverse Jacobian is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This gives the forward Jacobian \(\partial(x,y)/\partial(u,v)\) itself instead of taking its reciprocal.
This has the wrong sign on the reciprocal.
This makes a sign error inside the determinant before inverting, using \(ad+bc\) instead of \(ad-bc\).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
